data.title: Alibaba reaffirms open-source AI commitment as tech giant hails Qwen achievements
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s artificial intelligence strategy is defined by a commitment to open-source models, the company said on Tuesday, following a year of soaring adoption for its suite of AI services.
The statement came as Alibaba’s stock in New York rose more than 10 per cent on Monday, following news that its flagship Qwen family of AI models surpassed 700 million downloads on the developer platform Hugging Face, making it the world’s most widely adopted open-source AI system.
“A defining feature of Alibaba’s AI approach in 2025 has been its commitment to openness,” the company said. “Rather than gatekeeping capabilities, Alibaba has leaned into open-source software. This has helped developers and companies build faster and at lower cost.”
Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Open-source models from Alibaba and other Chinese AI companies had caught up or “even pulled ahead” of their US counterparts in capabilities and adoption, according to a Stanford University report published last month.
Alibaba hailed the importance of the open-source approach, which allowed “building with the community”. It cited the case of Nvidia’s Cosmos-Reason1-7B, a model platform for physical AI, which was post-trained based on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct for embodied reasoning and physical-world understanding.
The Hangzhou-based giant kicked off last year by announcing it would invest 380 billion yuan (US$53 billion) in AI and cloud infrastructure over the next three years. This commitment was expanded at September’s Apsara conference, in which CEO Eddie Wu Yongming laid out a road map to developing artificial superintelligence, sending the company’s stock to a four-year high.
Qwen downloads in December exceeded eight other major open-source developers combined, including OpenAI, DeepSeek and Meta, according to Chinese open-source AI expert Nathan Lambert, citing Hugging Face figures.
“This sets up the position where dethroning Qwen in adoption in 2026 looks impossible overall,” he said in his Interconnects newsletter on January 7.
Alibaba and its affiliates also ramped up integration of Qwen models into new consumer products last year, including the Qwen AI assistant app and Ant Group’s health app “A-Fu”.
Charlene Liu, HSBC’s head of internet and gaming research for Asia-Pacific, said in a research note on Friday that Alibaba’s cloud services would see continued revenue growth as long as AI-related demand persisted.
In November, group chairman Joe Tsai outlined Alibaba’s monetisation strategy for its open-source models at an event in Hong Kong, explaining that the company did not “make money from AI” per se.
“We run a cloud computing business,” he said. “When you run models, you need to have cloud infrastructure... If people are running AI and they happen to want to use Alibaba Cloud, we have a whole suite of products from storage to data management to security to networking to containers.”
Uncertainty remains over whether Alibaba and other Chinese AI developers will be able to keep up with their richer US counterparts given a relative lack of access to computational resources.
On Saturday, Qwen team leader Lin Junyang said at a major industry event in Beijing that he was pessimistic about China’s chances of surpassing the US in the near term, so long as domestic compute was tied up in daily delivery and not going towards foundational research.

data.title: South Korean prosecutors demand death penalty for Yoon Suk-yeol over martial law
data.description:
A Seoul court says an independent counsel has demanded a death sentence for former South Korean President Yoon Suk-yeol on rebellion charges in connection with his imposition of martial law in December 2024.
The Seoul Central District Court said independent counsel Cho Eun-suk’s team made the request at a hearing on Tuesday.
Removed from office last April, Yoon faces criminal trials over his short-lived martial law debacle and other scandals related to his time in office.

Charges that he directed a rebellion are the most significant ones.
The court is expected to deliver a verdict on Yoon in February.
Yoon has maintained that his decree was a desperate yet peaceful attempt to raise public awareness about what he considered the danger of the liberal opposition Democratic Party, which used its legislative majority to obstruct his agenda and complicate state affairs.
Yoon called the opposition-controlled parliament “a den of criminals” and “anti-state forces”.
But lawmakers rushed to object to the imposition of martial law in dramatic overnight scenes, and enough of them, including even those within Yoon’s ruling party, managed to enter an assembly hall to vote down Yoon’s decree.


Yoon’s decree, the first of its kind in more than 40 years in South Korea, brought armed troops into Seoul streets to encircle the assembly and enter election offices.
That evoked traumatic memories of dictatorships in the 1970s and 1980s, when military-backed rulers used martial law and other emergency decrees to station soldiers, tanks and armoured vehicles in public places to suppress pro-democracy protests.
Yoon’s decree and ensuing power vacuum plunged South Korea into political turmoil, halted the country’s high-level diplomacy and rattled its financial markets.
Yoon’s earlier vows to fight attempts to impeach and arrest him deepened the country’s political divide. In January last year, he became the country’s first sitting president to be detained.

data.title: Hong Kong activist loses legal fight over prison shorts ban for female inmates
data.description:
A jailed activist has lost a judicial review against a Hong Kong Correctional Services Department rule that prohibits female inmates from wearing shorts in summer, with the court ruling that the women’s standard prison uniform was carefully designed and did not constitute discrimination.
High Court judge Russell Coleman said on Tuesday that there was “little merit” in Chow Hang-tung’s argument that the department’s clothing policy violated her right to equality.
Under the existing rules, female prisoners must wear trousers rather than shorts, except during nighttime or physical training sessions.
Chow, 40, a barrister-turned-activist, has been held in custody since 2021 and is remanded at the Tai Lam Centre for Women.
She is awaiting trial under the national security law over her role as a former vice-chairwoman of the now-dissolved Hong Kong Alliance in Support of Patriotic Democratic Movements of China, which organised the city’s annual Tiananmen Square vigil.
Chow brought the judicial review on the grounds that female prisoners were treated less favourably than their male counterparts, who are permitted to wear shorts during the summer months.
However, Coleman said the department’s clothing policy was “customised for each gender” with regard to privacy and decency concerns for female inmates.
He believed the arrangement was made after careful consideration and that the court should defer to the commissioner’s judgment on the appropriate uniform for prisoners.

“The current set of uniform for [female prisoners] in summer daytime activities was the product of a carefully deliberated conclusion for the protection of them and with the agenda to address the privacy and decency concerns raised by them,” the judge said.
He noted that exemptions from wearing trousers in summer were available if the clothing had “actually or potentially” caused adverse effects on the inmates’ warmth or health conditions.
But he found that Chow had never raised any such complaint during her 136 medical consultations between 2021 and 2024, nor had she reported any health problems caused by wearing trousers in hot weather.
Coleman was satisfied that the department’s clothing rules were not inflexible and allowed for exceptions, rejecting Chow’s claim that she and other inmates were denied the opportunity to wear shorts in summer.
“Once those matters [in this case] are recognised, then the underlying complaint quickly loses what might at first blush appear to be its merit,” he said.
“I accept that in considering the overall context of [the department’s clothing policy], the items provided by default appear to have been customised for each gender in each season, and enforced equally between male and female prisoners.”
Chow’s legal challenge was therefore dismissed, and she was ordered to pay the costs of the proceedings.

data.title: Hong Kong corporate governance activist David Webb dies at 60
data.description:
David Webb, the corporate governance activist renowned for his free public database and his explosive “Enigma Network” exposé that rocked the Hong Kong stock exchange, has died aged 60 of metastatic prostate cancer.
A notice posted on one of his social media accounts confirmed his death on Tuesday. He is survived by his wife and two children.
“It is with great sadness that we share that David M. Webb MBE passed away peacefully in Hong Kong on Tuesday January 13, 2026 from metastatic prostate cancer. David will be missed by family, many friends, and supporters. The family request privacy at this difficult time,” the post read.
In 2020, he disclosed that he had been diagnosed with an aggressive form of metastatic prostate cancer. One of his final wishes, he said at the time, was to live beyond the age of 60.
His last post on Substack was titled “Government strongly condemns activities of the so-called Santa Claus”– a parody of the strongly worded statements frequently issued by the authorities in response to foreign criticism.
In May last year, Webb addressed a full house at the city’s Foreign Correspondents’ Club in what was billed as his public farewell.
“I fought the disease as hard as I could and I’m still fighting, but there is an obvious end to it,” Webb said during the event. “I will die confident that I did my best, and Hong Kong is my home.”

Born in London in 1965 and adopted as a baby, Webb spent part of his childhood in Thailand after his father took a job with the United Nations in Bangkok, before returning to the UK to complete his schooling.
A gifted student, Webb began programming and selling his own video games through classified adverts in computer magazines at just 16 and published his first book soon after completing his A-levels.
After earning a degree in mathematics from Exeter College, Oxford, in 1986, he spent a short period in corporate finance in London before relocating to Hong Kong in 1991. What was originally a two-year secondment became a permanent move – the city remained his home for the rest of his life.
In 1998, aged 32, Webb launched webb-site.com, a platform he managed single-handedly and made freely accessible to the public. The website focused on Hong Kong’s corporate and economic governance, as well as business, finance, investment, legal and regulatory affairs.
It also tracked the membership of more than 800 statutory and advisory bodies, ranging from the Executive and Legislative Councils to the Dogs and Cats Classification Board. Last year, he said he had spent HK$10 million (US$1.3 million) of his own money to establish and run the venture.

In 2017, Webb identified a group of interconnected Hong Kong-listed companies he dubbed the “Enigma Network” that had suspicious cross-holdings and circular transactions.
That December, the offices of Convoy Global Holdings were raided in Hong Kong’s largest financial investigation in decades. Former executives and directors were arrested, and many related stocks subsequently collapsed.
Webb also served in several prominent public roles promoting shareholder and investor rights, including on the Takeovers and Mergers Panel, at Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing, and with the Securities and Futures Commission’s Public Shareholders Group.

Last June, he was appointed as a Member of the Order of the British Empire “for services to raising standards of corporate economic governance, particularly in Hong Kong”.
One of his final acts was to make a repository of the eebb-site online database freely available for download after the original servers went offline last October. He had earlier revealed that he had received more than 100 offers to operate the platform.
“I won’t allow anyone to carry on eebb-site.com itself, because I would not want it potentially polluted or corrupted with clickbait, crypto scams or worse, or used to advocate something of which I would not have approved,” he said in a blog post on March 17. “The editorial side of it has to die with me.”

data.title: Taiwan and the US launch joint production of 155mm artillery shells amid blockade fears
data.description:
Taiwan has begun co-producing 155mm (6.1-inch) artillery shells with the US, as the island moves to strengthen wartime ammunition resilience amid Beijing’s escalating military pressure and concern over supply disruptions in a high-intensity conflict.
Taiwan’s defence ministry on Monday said Taipei and Washington had launched early-stage joint production of a new type of 155mm howitzer ammunition.
Lieutenant General Lin Wen-hsiang, head of Taiwan’s Armaments Bureau, testified during a legislative session that the Ukraine war underscored the urgency of securing a sustained ammunition supply in the event of an armed conflict.
“For defence resilience and because the Ukraine war has demonstrated that 155mm howitzers are among the fastest-consumed artillery systems, we have already begun preliminary work with the United States, starting with joint production of 155mm howitzer ammunition,” Lin said.
“If successful, this will then be expanded to other weapons and munitions,” he added.

Details of the Taiwan-US arrangement remained classified, Lin said. He described the project as part of a broader push to enhance Taiwan’s “defence resilience”.
In a report submitted to the legislature on Monday, the defence ministry said Taiwan faced particular challenges in resupplying ammunition during wartime, when sea and air transport could be “disrupted by a blockade or missile strikes”.
As a result, the military was prioritising local production and deeper cooperation with allies to reduce reliance on external supply chains, according to the report.
Military sources said the joint production effort was closely linked to Taiwan’s planned acquisition of US-made M109A7 Paladin self-propelled howitzers, which have longer barrels and require higher chamber pressure than existing systems.
Taiwan’s home-grown ammunition was incompatible with the new platforms, prompting the need for production standards aligned with US specifications, the sources added.
Local media have reported that the defence ministry plans to allocate about NT$14 billion (US$442.6 million) to build a new automated artillery ammunition production line at the Armaments Bureau’s 202nd Factory, while upgrading existing facilities to increase output.
The new line is expected to focus primarily on 155mm ammunition, which is in global short supply, as well as other high-demand munitions, according to the reports.
The co-production push forms part of a broader defence-industrial expansion under the government’s proposed NT$1.25 trillion special military spending package announced by Taiwan’s leader, William Lai Ching-te, in November.
Part of the eight-year programme aims to boost deterrence by expanding ammunition stockpiles, strengthening production capacity and deepening defence cooperation with the US.
Beyond shell production, Taiwan is also seeking to overhaul how ammunition is stored and transported.
The island’s defence ministry has earmarked funds under the special resilience budget to upgrade ammunition depots, disperse stockpiles, expand underground storage and adopt mobile logistics to reduce vulnerability to missile strikes.




The co-production effort has drawn support from senior US lawmakers.
In August last year, US Senate Armed Services Committee chairman Roger Wicker said during a visit to Taipei that he believed joint weapons production between the US and Taiwan would eventually be realised, including for uncrewed systems.
“I think there’s going to be joint production and joint efforts,” Wicker, a Mississippi Republican, told reporters, adding that Washington was open to innovation and proposals from Taiwan.
But US defence policymakers have urged pragmatism.
In November, Alexander Velez-Green, then the nominee for US deputy undersecretary of defence for policy, said the co-production should focus on “less technically complex capabilities for which Taiwan has existing workforce and production infrastructure”.
Formal co-production agreements often involved complicated and lengthy negotiations with partners and industry, Velez-Green said.
He argued that Taiwan should prioritise projects that can be delivered quickly to bolster deterrence and support a “denial defence” strategy.
Rupert Hammond-Chambers, president of the US-Taiwan Business Council, said in September that while momentum for co-production was strong at senior levels in both governments, execution remained highly complex.
This was especially the case for technology transfer approvals and dealing with any surplus production beyond Taiwan’s own needs, he added.
Meanwhile, Beijing has condemned the deepening US-Taiwan military cooperation.
Mainland China’s defence ministry has repeatedly said it “firmly opposes” any form of military collaboration between the US and Taiwan, warning that such moves violate the one-China principle and risk destabilising the Taiwan Strait.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Virginia Lo to resume quest for another personal best at Hong Kong Marathon
data.description:
Hong Kong distance runner Virginia Lo Ying-chiu has targeted another personal best at Sunday’s Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon (SCHKM), carrying on from breaking her best times on several occasions in 2025.
“Both my training progress and physical condition feel good, and I hope to run faster than last year’s Hong Kong Marathon and also break the PB set at the National Games – if the weather and other conditions are favourable,” Lo said.
Lo won the local women’s full marathon title last year in a personal best of two hours, 39 minutes and 56 seconds, finishing eighth overall among the women and becoming one of only two active Hong Kong female runners to break two hours and 40 minutes.
At the National Games in November, she improved that mark to 2:39:23 and had over the year lowered several other bests, including over 1,500m and 5,000m on the track, and in the half-marathon at the Gold Coast Marathon, where she ran 1:14:58.
Having run only her third full marathon last year and still feeling “fairly new” to the distance at that time, Lo said the recent results had boosted her confidence.

“Performing well at the National Games has boosted my confidence not just for the Hong Kong Marathon but for my marathon future as well,” Lo said. “To be honest, I still do not have much experience handling the full-marathon distance and a lot of the knowledge is still quite new to me. How to train and how to recover both need further exploration and study.”
With two full marathons in Hong Kong now behind her, Lo said the city’s notoriously steep course with many ups and downs no longer felt like a “devil”.
Lo said she had experienced both good and bad races on the Hong Kong Marathon route. “Besides personal form on the day, weather is a crucial factor,” she said. “Last year’s cool conditions made it feel less punishing, even though the long climb after the Western Harbour Crossing remained a stern test.
“Experience from the National Games and the Hong Kong half-marathon championships has taught me how to handle the hills and distribute my pace,” Lo added. “I am now more familiar with every section and less nervous going into this year’s race.”
Lo said her race strategy included staying calm when fatigue sets in. “Do not panic when the pace starts to bite. Use self-talk to convince yourself you can keep going,” Lo said.

The 28-year-old, who quit a stable teaching job more than three years ago to become a full-time athlete, said working with Japanese coach Shinetsu Murao over the past year had changed her outlook on training and competition.
She had learned to commit wholeheartedly to each race and not to dwell too much on how hard the process feels, she added.
Lo’s boyfriend, 2021 Hong Kong Marathon champion Eric Wong Kai-lok, will also return to the full distance this year.
Wong, who won the half-marathon title alongside Lo in 2023, hopes to better his personal best of 2:25:31 and will toe the line with a mindset of “wanting to win”.

data.title: Indonesia hails ‘99.9% successful’ free meals scheme amid food safety, oversight concerns
data.description:
Indonesia’s ambitious free nutritious meals programme has fed more than 55 million people in its first year, a scale-up President Prabowo Subianto has hailed as a major step in tackling childhood malnutrition, even as critics warn that rapid expansion has come at the expense of oversight, food safety and fiscal discipline.
Launched with fanfare last year, the scheme provides free meals to schoolchildren, infants and pregnant women, with the government framing it as a long-term investment in human capital and economic growth.
In its first year, the programme reached 55.1 million recipients and was overseen by the National Nutrition Agency, or BGN, which has partnered with more than 17,000 kitchens.
The Prabowo administration allocated 71 trillion rupiah (US$4.2 billion) for the programme last year, of which 51.5 trillion rupiah had been spent as of January 7, according to the finance ministry.
This year, the government has set aside 335 trillion rupiah, or 8.7 per cent of the total state budget, to expand coverage to 82.9 million people, including breastfeeding mothers and elderly citizens.

Prabowo has called the programme “strategic” to address malnutrition in up to 30 per cent of Indonesian children.
“[Many Indonesian children] are clearly malnourished, stunted, very weak, and have abnormal growth. It turns out that tens of millions of Indonesian children go to school without breakfast. Many of them only eat rice and leaves,” Prabowo said during a cabinet briefing on January 6, which marked the programme’s first anniversary.
While the programme was “99.9 per cent successful”, the president said his administration would continue to address any shortcomings.
Food safety issues
Critics have dismissed the government’s success claim, saying the scheme’s rapid expansion means not enough attention has been paid to food safety, nutrition guidance and oversight.
“If I could rate it, it’s an ‘F’. If I were a lecturer, and this programme was a research proposal, I would [deny] the proposal as it raised a lot of questions,” Diah Saminarsih, founder and chief executive of the Centre for Indonesia’s Strategic Development Initiatives, told This Week in Asia.
She called for a “temporary halt” of the programme to allow for evaluation.


Mass food poisoning cases have been continuously reported since the scheme began. As of December 23, more than 20,000 victims had fallen ill after eating meals from the programme, according to the Indonesian Education Monitoring Network, or JPPI.
So far this month, that number had reached 1,000, including the more than 800 students in an Islamic boarding school in Central Java who fell ill after eating “smelly” meals, a representative from the school told news outlet Detik on Sunday.
“If the president and the National Nutrition Agency continue to push this programme without a full audit and strict food safety [standards], the country is risking the lives of its own children. Children are not guinea pigs for policy,” said Ubaid Matraji, national coordinator at JPPI.
Annis Catur Adi, a nutritionist from the University of Airlangga in East Java, called on the government to involve “nutritionists and experts on mass food distribution”.
“In one case, the meal includes cartons of milk that are actually sugar water with milk flavouring. It clearly violates nutritional provisions,” he said.
The programme has also been criticised for a lack of standard operating procedures, which resulted in recent food distribution controversies such as the vague halal certification for food trays and a horror crash in a Jakarta school.
BGN had last week claimed that food poisoning cases had “significantly decreased” since August due to tighter requirements for hygiene and sanitation certificates for the kitchens, but noted that it could not guarantee zero incidents this year.

Massive budget
Analysts have also questioned the 335 trillion rupiah funding for the free meals scheme this year, given that the country is still reeling from the climate disaster that struck the provinces of Aceh, North Sumatra, and West Sumatra.
“The budget for the programme must be recalculated, and some must be allocated for the rehabilitation and reconstruction of Aceh and Sumatra,” Diah said.
A December report by the Centre of Economic and Law Studies think tank called the scheme’s massive funding “predatory”, as it took up 83.4 per cent of the education sector’s total budget this year.
The report said the money could otherwise have been used to “increase teacher incentives, improve the quality of educational assistance, and improve basic school facilities and infrastructure”.
Ubaid of JPPI said the budget should be used to “eliminate school fees”, as mandated by the constitutional court.
“This could be addressed with 75 trillion rupiah from the education budget. Currently, 4.1 million children remain out of school. This is not simply a policy omission, but a violation of the constitution,” he said.

Impact on growth
Prabowo has said Indonesia is on track to meet his ambitious target of 8 per cent economic growth, in part thanks to the free meals drive that could “create 1.5 million jobs” to boost the gross domestic product by “3 per cent”.
Data from the Ministry of Finance also showed that 741,985 workers had been employed by more than 17,000 kitchens throughout Indonesia as of December.
A recent calculation by the Institute for Development of Economics and Finance (Indef) think tank, however, found that the programme would only boost Indonesia’s GDP by up to 0.70 per cent by 2040, and then “gradually decrease and converge to zero in the long term”.
While the initiative helped drive employment, the benefits were “not reflected in real wage increases, but rather in increased actual welfare and consumption, thereby increasing the efficiency of labour productivity”, said Rizal Taufikurahman, head of the centre of macroeconomics and finance at Indef.
He urged Jakarta to limit the number of recipients, including by prioritising age groups and regions with the highest risk of malnutrition, “so that every rupiah spent produces a higher productivity impact”.

data.title: Trump’s audacious imperialism will only propel China’s rise
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s abduction of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro has been held up as an example of American military power. As Venezuela is not a producer of fentanyl – despite the United States accusing Maduro of drug trafficking – Trump has pivoted his attention to Venezuela’s oil reserves, the largest in the world.
Trump has commanded Venezuela to turn over 30 to 50 million barrels of sanctioned oil, which he said would be sold at market value and the proceeds from which he would control. That would only be the first tranche of indefinite shipments, with Trump saying his control of the money would “ensure it is used to benefit the people of Venezuela and the United States”.
Trump’s move is intended to kill five birds with one stone. First, it removed an anti-American leader from the US sphere of influence. Second, it secured Venezuela’s oil bonanza and control of its government. Third, it severed Venezuela’s economic dependency on China. Fourth, it cut essential oil supplies to Cuba in the hope of triggering Cuba’s economic implosion.
Last but not least, it set an example for the rest of South and Central America, paving the way for US dominance over the entire continent. This harks back to the 19th-century Monroe Doctrine, aimed at preventing hostile foreign influence throughout the western hemisphere.
Trump said during a recent interview with The New York Times that the only thing that could stop him was his “own morality”. In addition to his ambition to take control of Greenland and decision to withdraw the US from 66 international organisations via executive order, this is evidence of brazen predatory hegemony writ large.
How is Beijing responding to this overturn of the global order and trade norms, including the threat to China’s substantial investment in Venezuela? To understand the dynamics involved, some key realities need to be grasped upfront.

Most of Venezuela’s oil is the dirtiest, extra-heavy variety, and thanks to the “flaring” process of burning off excess natural gas it creates far more greenhouse gases than conventional oil. Decades of mismanagement and corruption have rendered many Venezuelan extraction facilities inoperative. As a result, the executives of US oil majors have had a largely guarded response to Trump’s urging that they invest more in the country.
Beijing is by far the largest buyer of Venezuela’s oil, though its imports only represent about 4 per cent of China’s total oil imports. Oil shipments have been used to repay Chinese loans to Caracas, with US$60 billion in loans now paid down to around US$10 billion. Walking away from a Venezuela in crisis, even if it brings some losses, is not exactly a nightmare for China.
In any case, Trump says he is ready to continue selling Venezuelan oil to China and Russia. He appears to be committed to running Venezuela by remote control. US failures in Iraq and Afghanistan are cold comfort to a country beset by cartels, insurgents and deep-rooted vested interests.
Regardless of Trump’s imperialism, China’s global supply chain dominance cannot be readily dislodged, including from South America. China’s Belt and Road Initiative has built transcontinental transport, telecommunication and digital infrastructure across Asia, Central Asia, Europe, the Middle East, Africa and South America, including a “digital silk road” which knows no physical boundaries.


A case in point is Peru’s Chancay Port, opened by President Xi Jinping and his Peruvian counterpart in November 2024 to serve as a major gateway for direct trade between South America, China and the rest of Asia. The port is capable of serving ultra-large container ships incompatible with the Panama Canal.
China has become the largest trading partner for a wide variety of countries. By 2018, around 130 countries traded more with China than they did with the US, and more than two-thirds of those countries traded more than twice as much with China. Almost everything manufactured these days is either made in China or has China’s components, processes and global logistics at its core.
Underpinning Beijing’s confidence to repel Trump’s sledgehammer approach to doing business is Xi’s prescience since Trump first gained the White House in focusing heavily on technological self-reliance and military advancement. China’s successful launch of the Tiangong space station, its artificial intelligence breakthrough with DeepSeek, its display of state-of-the-art military hardware during the National Day parade and its global leverage in rare earths are a check on Trump’s tariff madness. This has paved the way for Trump’s push for an April meeting with Xi in Beijing following their summit on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation gathering in South Korea.


China is following international trade norms, offering mutually beneficial and more stable and reliable relationships and promoting its vision of an inclusive, multipolar world order with its Global Development Initiative, Global Security Initiative and Global Civilisation Initiative. Countries across the globe are scrambling to hedge their bets with a more predictable and reliable China, ideological and other differences and scepticism notwithstanding.
Beijing is pushing ahead with launching its 15th five-year plan. This is expected to include opening up China’s transport, energy and financial sectors, boosting domestic consumption, improving people’s livelihoods, expanding regional and bilateral trade and investment agreements and accelerating internationalisation of the renminbi. This is all calculated to promote a fairer, more inclusive and cooperative international economic order.
In sum, Trump’s audacious imperialism only serves to turbocharge the global hedging momentum, propelling rather than slowing China’s rise.

data.title: Republican bill pushes to make Greenland 51st US state amid Trump’s bold arctic move
data.description:
A Republican congressman from Florida introduced a bill on Monday to annex Greenland and make it the 51st US state as President Donald Trump threatens to seize the autonomous Danish territory.
Trump has said the United States will take Greenland “one way or the other”, floating the idea of buying the territory or taking it by force, despite strong pushback from Denmark, a Nato ally.
Representative Randy Fine said his new legislation would authorise Trump “to take whatever steps necessary to annex or acquire Greenland”.

It would also require the Trump administration to send a report to Congress detailing changes to federal law that would allow the Arctic island to become a US state.
“Greenland is not a distant outpost we can afford to ignore – it is a vital national security asset,” Fine said in a statement.
Trump has insisted that Greenland, rich in rare earth mineral resources, needs to be brought under US control, arguing the territory is crucial for national security.

SOON pic.twitter.com/XU6VmZxph3
— Katie Miller (@KatieMiller) January 3, 2026

He has said Russia or China will take Greenland if the US does not.
Democrats and Republicans alike have pushed back on his rhetoric, and Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen has warned that an armed attack on Greenland from the US would spell the end of Nato, the 32-member military alliance.
Nato and Greenland’s government on Monday said they intended to work on strengthening the defence of the territory – a key concern cited by Trump.

data.title: Suspected scam boss linked to South Korean student’s brutal killing arrested in Thailand
data.description:
A Chinese national suspected of running a Cambodia-based scam operation linked to the killing of a South Korean university student has been arrested during a raid on a luxury house in Thailand, authorities said.
The suspect, identified by South Korean officials as Han Mo, was captured in Pattaya by a joint Thai–South Korean task force formed after the discovery of the student’s body last year sparked international outrage.
“In close cooperation with Thai authorities, we arrested Han Mo, 42, a Chinese national on [January 7] in Pattaya, Thailand. He is suspected of involvement in the murder of a South Korean university student,” the South Korean Ministry of Justice, police and intelligence agency said in a joint statement on Friday, according to The Chosun Daily.


The 22-year-old student, surnamed Park, was found dead in a car near Bokor Mountain in Cambodia’s Kampot province on August 8, last year. An autopsy concluded that he had suffered beatings and torture before dying from blunt force trauma.
Park’s murder came weeks after he had flown to Cambodia to take up what was reported to be a part-time job, and intensified scrutiny of scam compounds operating in the country.
Such compounds have been linked to organised crime networks that lure foreign nationals with promises of legitimate work, then detain them and force them to carry out online fraud.
South Korean, Thai and Cambodian authorities later formed a joint task force to dismantle the rings.
In October, Cambodian police arrested three Chinese nationals suspected of involvement in Park’s killing.
A month later, Li Guanghao, described by South Korean authorities as a Korean-Chinese national suspected of torturing and killing the student, was arrested in South Korea.
Han, who South Korea’s justice ministry described as the overall boss of the scam complex, is also referred to by officials as Korean-Chinese.
With the help of Chinese and South Korean accomplices, he is suspected of luring South Koreans to Cambodia between May and July last year by offering lucrative part-time jobs, the ministry said.
Once there, the victims were allegedly beaten, tortured and threatened at gunpoint to carry out their captors’ orders, including handing over bank account passwords and scamming other South Koreans online.
Han fled to Thailand shortly after Park’s body was discovered, Thai media outlet Thai Examiner reported.
South Korean authorities later traced him to a house in Pattaya and tipped off their Thai counterparts, who moved in to arrest him on Thursday.
During the raid, police found two other Chinese nationals at the property.

One was questioned and later released, while the second was arrested and charged with illegal drug consumption after acting erratically and admitting to using crystal meth, according to Thai authorities.
Investigators said they found no evidence linking the case to Thai victims.
South Korea has requested that Han be extradited to face charges.
“We will request the extradition of the criminal from Thai authorities and strive to repatriate him to South Korea,” the justice ministry told The Chosun Daily. “We will continue to cooperate with domestic and international agencies to track down and repatriate both South Korean and foreign criminals involved in the university student murder case.”
On Monday, South Korea’s presidential office announced that another joint operation by Cambodian and South Korean police had led to the arrest of 26 people in Phnom Penh.
The suspects were accused of running online scams and sex crimes targeting South Koreans.
The group had allegedly extorted about 26.7 billion won (US$18 million) from 165 South Koreans by impersonating police officers or financial regulators, Reuters reported.
Some female victims were also forced to film explicit videos and take sexually suggestive photographs for distribution, presidential spokeswoman Kang Yu-jung said, declining to provide further details.
